core_meaning: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる covers a wide family of 'upward' meanings: physical movement upward (going up stairs, rising), increases in numbers or levels (prices, temperature, grades), and entering an enclosed space like a house (stepping inside from the entrance). It also appears in expressions for finishing something (rain stopping, work wrapping up) and for feeling nervous.

sentences.jp: <ruby>靴<rt>くつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>脱<rt>ぬ</rt></ruby>いで、どうぞ<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってください。
sentences.translation: Please take off your shoes and come in.
sentences.notes: This shows the 'entering a Japanese home from the genkan' use — one of the most culturally important uses of <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる.

sentences.jp: さいきん、ガソリンの<ruby>値段<rt>ねだん</rt></ruby>がまた<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がったね。
sentences.translation: Gas prices went up again recently, huh.
sentences.notes: Shows the 'increase in price or level' sense, common in everyday conversation with the sentence-final ね.

sentences.jp: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がったら、<ruby>公園<rt>こうえん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>こう。
sentences.translation: Once the rain stops, let's go to the park.
sentences.notes: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is a fixed idiom meaning 'the rain clears up' — this 'something finishes/ends' sense is worth memorizing separately.

sentences.jp: <ruby>発表<rt>はっぴょう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>はいつも<ruby>緊張<rt>きんちょう</rt></ruby>して、あがってしまう。
sentences.translation: I always get nervous and flustered before presentations.
sentences.notes: あがる (often written in kana) means to get stage fright or become flustered — a distinct idiomatic sense separate from the physical 'rise' meanings.

sentences.jp: <ruby>成績<rt>せいせき</rt></ruby>を<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げるために、<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby><ruby>復習<rt>ふくしゅう</rt></ruby>することが<ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>だ。
sentences.translation: In order to improve your grades, it is important to review every day.
sentences.notes: Note: this sentence uses <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる (the transitive partner — 'to raise something'), showing the contrast: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is intransitive (something rises on its own), while <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる is transitive (you raise something).

dont_use: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is intransitive, so don't use it when you need a direct object — say '<ruby>音量<rt>おんりょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる' (raise the volume), not '<ruby>音量<rt>おんりょう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる' when you are the one doing the raising. Also avoid confusing it with <ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る, which is used for climbing mountains or stairs with effort and implies a longer physical journey upward. Finally, don't use <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる for the sun or abstract concepts rising — <ruby>昇<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る is preferred for the sun rising.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>上<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る emphasizes the process of physically climbing something tall, like a mountain or a long staircase — it implies ongoing upward travel and effort. <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる focuses on the result of arriving at a higher point, or is used for abstract/numerical rises. For example, '<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>に<ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る' means to climb a mountain (the journey), while '<ruby>気温<rt>きおん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる' means the temperature rises (a change in level).

frequency: Very common in daily speech across all registers. The 'price/level rises,' 'enter a home,' and 'rain stops' uses appear constantly in natural Japanese. The stage-fright sense (あがる) is also widely understood colloquially.
anki_hint: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is intransitive ('something goes up on its own') — prices, temperature, and even you yourself stepping up into a house all '<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる,' but when you actively raise something, switch to <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる.
